content.problemStatement:
**This is the easy version of the general Tree Colouring task. You only need to find a valid colouring of the whole tree instead of checking if every prefix is colourable. This is only present in Divisions 222, 333 and 444. This version is worth 505050 points.**

You have a tree with NNN nodes numbered 111 to NNN.

You want to assign each node a colour CiC_iCi​ (1≤Ci≤N1 \le C_i \le N1≤Ci​≤N) such that the following conditions hold:


Each colour XXX appears either 000 times or ≥2\ge 2≥2 times.
Consider all possible pairs (A,B)(A, B)(A,B) such that 1≤A<B≤N1 \le A < B \le N1≤A<B≤N and CA=CBC_A = C_BCA​=CB​:

Each edge should lie on the unique shortest path of **exactly** one such pair.


Let us call a tree **good** if it is possible to assign colours satisfying the above property.

In this version, you are given a tree TTT of NNN nodes represented by an array PPP meaning there is a edge between iii and PiP_iPi​ (for 2≤i≤N2 \le i \le N2≤i≤N), i.e. there exists an edge between 222 and P2P_2P2​, 333 and P3P_3P3​, ..., NNN and PNP_NPN​. Here, PiP_iPi​ is the parent of iii. P1P_1P1​ is not given since 111 has no parent.

If the tree is **good**, we require you to output an appropriate colour scheme for that tree.

If there are multiple valid colour schemes, you should **minimize** the number of distinct colours used. If there are still multiple valid schemes, all will be accepted.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line contains NNN - the number of nodes in the tree.
The second line contains N−1N - 1N−1 integers - P2,P3,...,PNP_2, P_3, ..., P_NP2​,P3​,...,PN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output the following:



if it is possible to obtain a valid colouring scheme, output NNN integers - C1,C2,...,CNC_1, C_2, ..., C_NC1​,C2​,...,CN​ (1≤Ci≤N1 \le C_i \le N1≤Ci​≤N)
otherwise, output −1-1−1.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : The tree with 222 nodes and (1,2)(1, 2)(1,2) being the only edge is good, as depicted by the colouring C=[2,2]C = [2, 2]C=[2,2]. There is only one edge and one pair, and the edge lies on the shortest path of that pair.
Test Case 2 : It can be shown that the tree of 444 nodes is not good. For example, C=[1,2,1,2]C = [1, 2, 1, 2]C=[1,2,1,2] makes the edge (2,3)(2, 3)(2,3) occur on the shortest paths of 222 different pairs.
Test Case 3 : The tree of 444 nodes is good as shown by the colouring C=[1,3,1,3]C = [1, 3, 1, 3]C=[1,3,1,3].
